description.task: Implement the SQL tables defined in an ER diagram description and write queries to demonstrate the relationships.
description.context: An ER (Entity-Relationship) Diagram shows entities (tables), their attributes (columns), and relationships (foreign keys) visually. This assignment translates a textual ER description into SQL.
instructions: Run all CREATE and INSERT statements.
Run the final query to see instructor-course assignments.
Write an additional query: show all students enrolled in 'Database Fundamentals'.
In a comment, explain the difference between a one-to-many and a many-to-many relationship.
description.hint: For the additional query, JOIN enrollment → student → course WHERE course title = 'Database Fundamentals'.
description.expectedOutput:
Instructor-course: Prof.Jones/Web Dev, Prof.Smith/DB Fundamentals, Prof.Smith/Algorithms
DB Fundamentals students: Alice(A), Bob(B)

description.task: Implement one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many relationships in SQL and query each.
instructions: Run all CREATE and INSERT statements.
Run all three queries at the bottom.
In comments, label which relationship type (1:1, 1:N, M:N) each table structure implements.
Explain: why does UNIQUE on emp_id in employee_profile enforce the 1:1 relationship?
description.hint: UNIQUE on a FK column means each parent row can appear at most once — exactly what you need for 1:1. Without UNIQUE, multiple profile rows could reference the same employee.
description.expectedOutput:
1:1 query: Alice/Senior Developer, Bob/Marketing Lead
1:N query: Engineering:2, Marketing:1
M:N query: Alice/SQL101, Alice/Python101, Bob/SQL101

title: Design an ER Diagram for a Library

description.task: Design and implement a simple library database from an ER description, then write queries to test all relationships.
description.context: Designing from scratch: identify entities, attributes, relationships and cardinalities before writing any SQL.
instructions: Run all CREATE and INSERT statements.
Write a NEW query: list all books with their authors.
Write another query: show members who have never borrowed a book (LEFT JOIN).
description.hint: Books with authors: JOIN book → book_author → author. Members who never borrowed: LEFT JOIN member → loan WHERE loan.loan_id IS NULL.
description.expectedOutput:
On loan: Alice/Clean Code/2026-01-15
All books with authors: 3 rows
Members never borrowed: 0 (both have loans)
